title: Introduction to Cells
content_html:
Introduction to Cells

What a cell is

A cell is the smallest unit of life that can carry out all the basic functions needed to survive. Some organisms are made of one cell (unicellular), while others are made of many cells (multicellular).

Two big cell families

  • Prokaryotes: no nucleus; DNA is in the cytoplasm (e.g., bacteria).
  • Eukaryotes: have a nucleus and many internal compartments (organelles) (e.g., animals, plants, fungi).

Why cells matter in biotechnology

Biotechnology often means using cells (or molecules from cells) to make products or solve problems: insulin made by engineered bacteria, vaccines produced in cell cultures, or enzymes used in industry.

Key takeaways

  • Cells are the basic unit of life.
  • Prokaryotes vs eukaryotes is a fundamental distinction.
  • Biotech works with cells, genes, and cell-made molecules.

Quick check

  1. What is the main difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?

    Show answer

    Eukaryotic cells have a nucleus (and many membrane-bound organelles); prokaryotic cells do not.

  2. Give one biotech application that uses cells.

    Show answer

    Example: producing insulin using genetically modified bacteria.

title: What Is Life?
content_html:
What Is Life?

Common properties of life

Living systems share a set of properties. Different textbooks list them differently, but the core ideas are:

  • Organization: cells and structures are ordered.
  • Energy use: living systems take in energy and transform it (metabolism).
  • Homeostasis: they keep internal conditions stable (temperature, pH, etc.).
  • Growth and reproduction: they produce new cells/organisms.
  • Information: genetic information (DNA) stores instructions.
  • Evolution: populations change over time.

Why definitions are tricky

Some systems look alive but are debated (e.g., viruses). Viruses have genetic material and evolve, but they usually need a host cell to reproduce and do not have their own metabolism.

Key takeaways

  • Life is defined by a combination of properties, not a single feature.
  • DNA stores information; metabolism manages energy; homeostasis keeps stability.
  • Viruses challenge the boundary between living and non-living.

Quick check

  1. Name three properties of life.

    Show answer

    Example: metabolism, homeostasis, evolution.

  2. Why are viruses often not considered fully living?

    Show answer

    They typically cannot reproduce or carry out metabolism on their own; they rely on host cells.

title: Atoms & Molecules
content_html:
Atoms & Molecules

Atoms: the building blocks

An atom has a nucleus (protons + neutrons) and electrons around it. The number of protons defines the element (carbon, oxygen, etc.).

Molecules and compounds

A molecule is formed when atoms are held together by chemical bonds. When different elements bond together, we often call the result a compound (e.g., H2O).

Why electrons matter

Most chemistry in biology happens because atoms try to reach stable electron configurations. This drives bond formation and explains why some atoms form more bonds than others.

Key takeaways

  • Elements are defined by number of protons.
  • Molecules form through bonds between atoms.
  • Electron behavior drives bonding and reactivity in biology.

Quick check

  1. What defines an element?

    Show answer

    The number of protons in the atom’s nucleus.

  2. What is a molecule?

    Show answer

    Two or more atoms bonded together.

title: Chemical Bonds (Ionic, Covalent, Hydrogen)
content_html:
Chemical Bonds (Ionic, Covalent, Hydrogen)

Covalent bonds

Covalent bonds form when atoms share electrons. They are strong and create stable molecules like sugars, proteins, and DNA.

In polar covalent bonds, electrons are shared unequally (e.g., O–H in water), creating partial charges (δ− and δ+).

Ionic interactions

Ions form when atoms gain or lose electrons. Oppositely charged ions attract each other (ionic interaction). In cells, ionic interactions are often weakened because water surrounds and stabilizes ions.

Hydrogen bonds and weak forces

Hydrogen bonds occur when a hydrogen attached to an electronegative atom (like O or N) is attracted to another electronegative atom. Individually weak, but collectively powerful — for example, they stabilize DNA base pairing and protein structure.

Key takeaways

  • Covalent bonds are strong; hydrogen bonds are weaker but crucial in biology.
  • Polarity creates partial charges, enabling interactions in water.
  • Ionic interactions matter in salts and many biological processes.

Quick check

  1. Why is water polar?

    Show answer

    Because oxygen pulls shared electrons more strongly than hydrogen, creating partial charges.

  2. Give one biological example where hydrogen bonds matter.

    Show answer

    DNA base pairing or protein folding.

title: pH & Buffers
content_html:
pH & Buffers

What pH measures

pH describes how acidic or basic a solution is. Formally, it relates to the concentration of hydrogen ions (H+) in water.

  • Low pH (more H+) → acidic
  • High pH (less H+) → basic

Why pH matters for cells

Many proteins (especially enzymes) only work properly in a narrow pH range because pH changes can alter charges on amino acids and change protein shape.

Buffers: stability systems

A buffer resists pH change. It usually contains a weak acid and its conjugate base. Buffers are essential in blood and inside cells to keep conditions stable.

Key takeaways

  • pH is linked to hydrogen ion concentration.
  • Enzyme activity is strongly affected by pH.
  • Buffers help maintain stable pH in biological systems.

Quick check

  1. What happens to enzyme activity if pH changes too much?

    Show answer

    The enzyme may change shape/charge and lose activity.

  2. What is a buffer?

    Show answer

    A solution that resists changes in pH when small amounts of acid/base are added.

title: 2.3 — Amino Acids & Proteins
content_html:
Amino Acids & Proteins

Amino acids: the monomers

Proteins are built from amino acids. Each amino acid has an amino group, a carboxyl group, a hydrogen, and a variable R group that determines its properties.

From amino acids to polypeptides

Amino acids connect through peptide bonds to form a polypeptide chain. The sequence of amino acids (primary structure) influences how the protein folds.

Protein structure levels

  • Primary: amino acid sequence.
  • Secondary: local patterns like α-helices and β-sheets (often stabilized by hydrogen bonds).
  • Tertiary: 3D folding of one polypeptide.
  • Quaternary: multiple polypeptides assembled together.

Function depends on shape

Proteins perform many roles: enzymes, transport, signaling, structure. If shape changes (e.g., heat, extreme pH), function can be lost — this is denaturation.

Key takeaways

  • Proteins are polymers of amino acids connected by peptide bonds.
  • Structure (folding) determines function.
  • Denaturation can disrupt protein activity.

Quick check

  1. What is the R group?

    Show answer

    The variable side chain that gives each amino acid its unique properties.

  2. What does 'denaturation' mean?

    Show answer

    Loss of a protein’s normal shape (and usually its function).

title: 2.1 — Matter, Elements, Atoms & Isotopes
content_html:
Matter, Elements, Atoms & Isotopes

Matter and elements

Matter is anything that has mass and takes up space. An element is a substance that cannot be broken down by chemical reactions (e.g., carbon, nitrogen).

Atoms and isotopes

Atoms of the same element always have the same number of protons. Isotopes are atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons.

Some isotopes are unstable and are called radioisotopes. They can be used in medicine (imaging, cancer therapy) and biology (tracing molecules).

Ions in biology

When an atom gains or loses electrons, it becomes an ion. Ions are important in nerve signaling, muscle contraction, and maintaining osmotic balance.

Key takeaways

  • Isotopes: same element, different neutrons.
  • Radioisotopes are useful tools in medicine and research.
  • Ions are central to many cellular processes.

Quick check

  1. What is an isotope?

    Show answer

    Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons.

  2. Name one use of radioisotopes.

    Show answer

    Medical imaging or tracing molecules in experiments.

title: 2.2 — Chemical Bonds & Why Water Matters
content_html:
Chemical Bonds & Why Water Matters

Water’s special properties

Because water is polar, it forms hydrogen bonds. This gives water key properties:

  • Excellent solvent for ionic and polar substances.
  • High heat capacity (temperature changes slowly).
  • Cohesion/adhesion (surface tension, capillary action).

Hydrophobic effect

Nonpolar molecules do not mix well with water. Water tends to exclude them, causing nonpolar molecules to cluster together. This is the hydrophobic effect — essential for cell membranes and protein folding.

pH reminder

Water can split into H+ and OH−. Biological systems regulate pH carefully using buffers.

Key takeaways

  • Water’s polarity explains many biological behaviors.
  • Hydrophobic effect drives membrane formation and folding.
  • Buffers maintain stable pH.

Quick check

  1. Why is water a good solvent?

    Show answer

    Its polarity allows it to surround and stabilize ions and polar molecules.

  2. What is the hydrophobic effect?

    Show answer

    Nonpolar molecules cluster together in water to minimize disruption of hydrogen bonding.

title: 2.4 — Carbohydrates
content_html:
Carbohydrates

What they are

Carbohydrates are molecules made mainly of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen. They often serve as energy sources and structural materials.

Monosaccharides to polysaccharides

  • Monosaccharides: glucose, fructose.
  • Disaccharides: sucrose, lactose.
  • Polysaccharides: starch (plant storage), glycogen (animal storage), cellulose (plant structure).

Why structure matters

Small differences in how sugar units connect can change function dramatically. For example, starch and cellulose are both polymers of glucose, but different bond patterns make cellulose much harder to digest.

Key takeaways

  • Carbs can store energy and build structure.
  • Polysaccharides differ by linkage type and branching.
  • Bond type can change digestibility and function.

Quick check

  1. What is glycogen?

    Show answer

    A storage polysaccharide in animals.

  2. Why is cellulose hard to digest for humans?

    Show answer

    Its linkages require enzymes humans do not have.

title: 2.5 — Lipids
content_html:
Lipids

General idea

Lipids are mostly hydrophobic molecules. They are important for long-term energy storage, membrane structure, and signaling.

Main types

  • Fats (triglycerides): glycerol + fatty acids; energy storage.
  • Phospholipids: form cell membranes (hydrophilic head + hydrophobic tails).
  • Steroids: hormones like estrogen/testosterone; cholesterol affects membrane fluidity.

Saturated vs unsaturated

Saturated fats have no double bonds (often solid at room temp). Unsaturated fats have one or more double bonds (often liquid), which introduce bends and affect packing.

Key takeaways

  • Lipids are hydrophobic and include fats, phospholipids, and steroids.
  • Phospholipids build membranes via bilayers.
  • Saturation affects membrane fluidity and fat properties.

Quick check

  1. Which lipid forms membranes?

    Show answer

    Phospholipids.

  2. What does a double bond do in a fatty acid chain?

    Show answer

    It introduces a bend and reduces tight packing.

title: Quiz — Chapter 2 (6 questions)
content_html:
Quiz — Chapter 2: Biomolecules

Answer the questions, then open the answers to self-check.

  1. An isotope of an element differs in:

    • Number of protons
    • Number of neutrons
    • Number of electrons (always)
    • Chemical symbol
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Number of neutrons

    Isotopes have the same protons (same element) but different neutrons.

  2. Which bond is strongest in biological molecules?

    • Hydrogen bond
    • Covalent bond
    • Van der Waals interaction
    • Ionic interaction in water
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Covalent bond

    Covalent bonds involve shared electrons and are typically the strongest of these.

  3. The hydrophobic effect is most directly responsible for:

    • DNA base pairing
    • Protein synthesis
    • Membrane bilayer formation
    • Radioactive decay
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Membrane bilayer formation

    Hydrophobic tails cluster away from water, driving bilayer formation.

  4. Proteins are polymers made of:

    • Nucleotides
    • Amino acids
    • Monosaccharides
    • Fatty acids
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Amino acids

    Amino acids link by peptide bonds to form polypeptides.

  5. Cellulose is best described as:

    • Animal energy storage
    • Plant structural polysaccharide
    • A lipid hormone
    • A DNA base
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Plant structural polysaccharide

    Cellulose forms plant cell walls; its linkages make it rigid.

  6. Phospholipids have:

    • Two hydrophilic tails
    • A hydrophilic head and hydrophobic tails
    • No polar parts
    • Only one fatty acid tail
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: A hydrophilic head and hydrophobic tails

    This amphipathic structure is why they form bilayers in water.

title: 1.1 — What Is Biology? The Properties of Life
content_html:
What Is Biology? The Properties of Life

Biology in one sentence

Biology is the study of living systems — from molecules and cells to organisms and ecosystems — and how they interact and evolve.

Core properties of life

Living systems typically show:

  • Cellular organization (cells are the basic units).
  • Metabolism (energy transformation).
  • Homeostasis (internal stability).
  • Growth & reproduction.
  • Response to stimuli.
  • Genetic information (DNA) and evolution.

Biology + biotechnology

Biotechnology applies biological knowledge to create tools and products: diagnostics, drugs, crops, enzymes, and environmental solutions.

Key takeaways

  • Biology studies life at many scales.
  • Life is recognized by a combination of key properties.
  • Biotechnology applies biology to real-world problems.

Quick check

  1. What is the smallest unit of life?

    Show answer

    The cell.

  2. What is metabolism?

    Show answer

    All chemical reactions that manage energy and materials in a living system.

title: 1.2 — The Process of Science (Scientific Method)
content_html:
The Process of Science (Scientific Method)

The scientific method (simple version)

  1. Observation: notice a phenomenon.
  2. Question: what do we want to explain?
  3. Hypothesis: a testable explanation.
  4. Prediction: what should happen if the hypothesis is true?
  5. Experiment: test with controls and variables.
  6. Analysis: interpret data and decide if hypothesis is supported.

Controls and variables

A good experiment changes one variable at a time and compares it to a control condition. This helps attribute effects to the tested factor.

Correlation vs causation

Finding a relationship (correlation) does not automatically mean one causes the other. Causation requires stronger evidence, often through controlled experiments.

Key takeaways

  • Hypotheses must be testable and lead to predictions.
  • Controls make experiments interpretable.
  • Correlation ≠ causation.

Quick check

  1. What is a hypothesis?

    Show answer

    A testable explanation for an observation.

  2. Why do we need a control group?

    Show answer

    To compare and isolate the effect of the tested variable.

title: 1.3 — Levels of Biological Organization
content_html:
Levels of Biological Organization

From small to large

Biology spans many levels:

  • Atoms → molecules → organelles → cells
  • Tissues → organs → organ systems → organisms
  • Populations → communities → ecosystems → biosphere

Emergent properties

At each level, new properties appear that are not obvious from the parts alone. For example, individual molecules aren’t “alive,” but organized molecules in a cell create life-like behavior.

Why this matters in biotech

Biotech projects often move between levels: modifying a gene (molecular) to change protein function (molecular) to alter cell behavior (cellular) to affect an organism (whole-system).

Key takeaways

  • Biology is studied across nested levels of organization.
  • Emergent properties appear at higher levels.
  • Biotech connects molecular changes to cellular/organism outcomes.

Quick check

  1. What is an emergent property?

    Show answer

    A new property that arises from the interactions of parts at a higher level.

  2. Place these in order: cell, tissue, organ.

    Show answer

    Cell → tissue → organ.

title: 1.4 — Three Unifying Theories of Biology
content_html:
Three Unifying Theories of Biology

1) Cell Theory

  • All living things are made of cells.
  • The cell is the basic unit of life.
  • Cells come from pre-existing cells.

2) Gene Theory

Genes (DNA sequences) carry information that can be inherited. Gene expression (DNA → RNA → protein) connects genotype to phenotype.

3) Theory of Evolution

Populations change over time. Natural selection favors traits that improve survival/reproduction in a given environment. Evolution explains both similarities and diversity across life.

Key takeaways

  • Cell theory explains biological organization.
  • Gene theory explains inheritance and information flow.
  • Evolution explains unity and diversity across life.

Quick check

  1. Which theory explains inheritance?

    Show answer

    Gene theory.

  2. Which theory explains why species change over time?

    Show answer

    Evolution.

title: Quiz — Chapter 1 (5 questions)
content_html:
Quiz — Chapter 1: The Study of Life

Answer the questions, then open the answers to self-check.

  1. The basic unit of life is:

    • Atom
    • Cell
    • Tissue
    • Organ
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Cell

    Cells are the smallest units that carry out life functions.

  2. A hypothesis should be:

    • A personal opinion
    • Not testable
    • Testable and lead to predictions
    • Always true
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Testable and lead to predictions

    Scientific hypotheses must be testable using evidence.

  3. Which is the best example of a control?

    • Changing multiple variables at once
    • A comparison group without the tested change
    • Skipping measurements
    • Only using one sample
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: A comparison group without the tested change

    Controls help isolate the effect of the variable you test.

  4. Emergent properties are:

    • Properties of single atoms
    • New properties that appear at higher levels of organization
    • Always genetic
    • Only found in ecosystems
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: New properties that appear at higher levels of organization

    They result from interactions among parts.

  5. Which unifying theory explains unity and diversity of life?

    • Cell theory
    • Gene theory
    • Evolution
    • None
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Evolution

    Evolution explains both shared ancestry and differences across species.

title: 3.1 — Cell Theory + Prokaryotes vs Eukaryotes
content_html:
Cell Theory + Prokaryotes vs Eukaryotes

Cell theory recap

Cell theory states that all living things are made of cells, cells are the basic unit of life, and new cells arise from existing cells.

Prokaryotes

  • No nucleus; DNA in nucleoid region.
  • Generally smaller and simpler.
  • Examples: bacteria, archaea.

Eukaryotes

  • Have nucleus (DNA enclosed).
  • Have membrane-bound organelles (ER, Golgi, mitochondria, etc.).
  • Examples: animals, plants, fungi, protists.

Why this matters

Many biotech tools use bacteria because they grow fast and are easy to engineer, but some products require eukaryotic cells for correct folding or modifications.

Key takeaways

  • Prokaryotes lack nucleus; eukaryotes have nucleus and organelles.
  • Cell theory is a foundation of modern biology.
  • Different cell types are used for different biotech goals.

Quick check

  1. Where is DNA located in prokaryotes?

    Show answer

    In the nucleoid region (not inside a nucleus).

  2. Why might a biotech product need eukaryotic cells?

    Show answer

    For complex folding or modifications (like glycosylation).

title: 3.2 — Endomembrane System (ER, Golgi, Vesicles)
content_html:
Endomembrane System (ER, Golgi, Vesicles)

Big idea

The endomembrane system is a network of membranes that builds, modifies, and transports proteins and lipids.

Rough ER vs smooth ER

  • Rough ER: has ribosomes; makes proteins for secretion/membranes.
  • Smooth ER: makes lipids; detox; calcium storage.

Golgi apparatus

The Golgi modifies, sorts, and packages proteins/lipids into vesicles for delivery.

Vesicles and trafficking

Vesicles transport cargo between organelles and to/from the cell surface (exocytosis and endocytosis).

Key takeaways

  • ER makes proteins/lipids; Golgi modifies and ships them.
  • Vesicles are the delivery vehicles inside cells.
  • This system is central to secretion and membrane maintenance.

Quick check

  1. Which organelle modifies and sorts proteins for export?

    Show answer

    The Golgi apparatus.

  2. What is the difference between rough and smooth ER?

    Show answer

    Rough ER has ribosomes and makes proteins; smooth ER makes lipids and detoxifies.

title: 3.3 — Cytoskeleton & Cell Junctions
content_html:
Cytoskeleton & Cell Junctions

Cytoskeleton overview

The cytoskeleton gives shape, enables movement, and helps with internal transport. Main components:

  • Microtubules: tracks for transport; important in cell division.
  • Microfilaments (actin): cell shape; muscle contraction; cell crawling.
  • Intermediate filaments: mechanical strength.

Cell junctions (in animal tissues)

  • Tight junctions: seal between cells (prevent leakage).
  • Desmosomes: strong attachments (resist stress).
  • Gap junctions: communication channels.

Plant cell connections

Plants use plasmodesmata to connect cells and exchange materials.

Key takeaways

  • Cytoskeleton supports shape, transport, and movement.
  • Junctions connect cells mechanically and functionally.
  • Different organisms use different connection structures.

Quick check

  1. Which cytoskeleton component forms tracks for vesicle transport?

    Show answer

    Microtubules.

  2. What is a gap junction used for?

    Show answer

    Cell-to-cell communication via channels.

title: 3.4 — Membrane Transport (Passive)
content_html:
Membrane Transport (Passive)

Cell membranes are selective

The cell membrane is a phospholipid bilayer that controls what enters/exits the cell.

Diffusion and osmosis

  • Diffusion: movement from high → low concentration.
  • Osmosis: diffusion of water across a membrane.

Facilitated diffusion

Some molecules need help crossing the membrane through channel proteins or carriers. No energy is used because movement is still down a concentration gradient.

Tonicity

Hypotonic solution: water enters the cell. Hypertonic: water leaves the cell. Isotonic: no net water movement.

Key takeaways

  • Passive transport moves down gradients without energy.
  • Osmosis is water diffusion; tonicity predicts water direction.
  • Channels/carriers enable facilitated diffusion.

Quick check

  1. Does facilitated diffusion require ATP?

    Show answer

    No, it moves down a gradient without energy.

  2. What happens to an animal cell in a hypertonic solution?

    Show answer

    It loses water and shrinks.

title: 3.5 — Membrane Transport (Active + Vesicular)
content_html:
Membrane Transport (Active + Vesicular)

Active transport

Active transport uses energy (often ATP) to move substances against a gradient (low → high concentration). Transport proteins that do this are often called pumps.

Example: the sodium-potassium pump maintains ion gradients important for nerve impulses.

Co-transport

Sometimes a gradient created by one pump is used to drive transport of another molecule — this is secondary active transport.

Vesicular transport

  • Endocytosis: bringing material into the cell (phagocytosis, pinocytosis, receptor-mediated).
  • Exocytosis: exporting material out of the cell.

Key takeaways

  • Active transport uses energy to move against gradients.
  • Secondary active transport uses existing gradients.
  • Endocytosis/exocytosis move large cargo via vesicles.

Quick check

  1. What is the key difference between passive and active transport?

    Show answer

    Active requires energy and can move against gradients.

  2. Name one type of endocytosis.

    Show answer

    Phagocytosis, pinocytosis, or receptor-mediated endocytosis.

title: Quiz — Chapter 3 (6 questions)
content_html:
Quiz — Chapter 3: The Organization of Cells

Answer the questions, then open the answers to self-check.

  1. Which is true of prokaryotes?

    • They have a nucleus
    • They have membrane-bound organelles
    • Their DNA is not in a nucleus
    • They are always multicellular
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Their DNA is not in a nucleus

    Prokaryotes lack a nucleus.

  2. Rough ER is associated with:

    • Lipid synthesis
    • Protein synthesis for export/membranes
    • DNA replication
    • ATP production
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Protein synthesis for export/membranes

    Rough ER has ribosomes that synthesize proteins.

  3. The Golgi apparatus mainly:

    • Stores genetic information
    • Modifies and sorts proteins
    • Breaks down waste (always)
    • Performs photosynthesis
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Modifies and sorts proteins

    Golgi processes and packages cargo into vesicles.

  4. Microtubules are important for:

    • Cell division and transport tracks
    • Making ATP
    • DNA base pairing
    • Buffering pH
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Cell division and transport tracks

    They form spindle fibers and serve as tracks for motor proteins.

  5. Facilitated diffusion:

    • Uses ATP
    • Moves molecules against gradient
    • Uses membrane proteins but no ATP
    • Only moves water
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Uses membrane proteins but no ATP

    Movement is down the gradient; proteins help crossing.

  6. Endocytosis is:

    • Moving ions through channels
    • Exporting vesicles out
    • Importing material using vesicles
    • Diffusion of water
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Importing material using vesicles

    Endocytosis brings material into the cell.

title: 4.1 — Metabolism: Energy in Living Systems
content_html:
Metabolism: Energy in Living Systems

Metabolism = all reactions

Metabolism is the sum of all chemical reactions in a cell/organism. It includes:

  • Catabolism: breaking molecules down to release energy.
  • Anabolism: building molecules, which requires energy.

Energy and pathways

Cells manage energy through metabolic pathways — sequences of reactions where each step is controlled. This prevents wasting energy and allows regulation.

Coupling reactions

Cells often couple an energy-releasing reaction to an energy-requiring one. ATP is the most common “energy currency” used for this coupling.

Key takeaways

  • Catabolism releases energy; anabolism consumes energy.
  • Metabolic pathways are stepwise and regulated.
  • ATP helps couple reactions.

Quick check

  1. Is building a protein anabolic or catabolic?

    Show answer

    Anabolic (building).

  2. Why do cells use pathways instead of one big reaction?

    Show answer

    To control steps and capture energy efficiently.

title: 4.2 — Enzymes: Biological Catalysts
content_html:
Enzymes: Biological Catalysts

What enzymes do

Enzymes speed up reactions by lowering the activation energy. They do not change the final equilibrium — they just help the reaction reach it faster.

Active site and specificity

Substrates bind to an enzyme’s active site. Shape and chemical properties create specificity (lock-and-key / induced-fit ideas).

Factors affecting enzyme activity

  • Temperature: higher can increase rate until denaturation.
  • pH: affects charge and folding; each enzyme has an optimal pH.
  • Substrate concentration: rate increases until saturation.
  • Inhibitors: competitive or noncompetitive reduce activity.

Key takeaways

  • Enzymes lower activation energy and speed reactions.
  • Specificity comes from the active site.
  • Temperature, pH, concentration, and inhibitors change activity.

Quick check

  1. Do enzymes get consumed in reactions?

    Show answer

    No, they can be reused.

  2. What does a competitive inhibitor do?

    Show answer

    Competes with substrate for the active site.

title: 4.3 — ATP: The Energy Currency
content_html:
ATP: The Energy Currency

ATP basics

ATP (adenosine triphosphate) stores usable energy in its phosphate bonds. When ATP loses a phosphate (ATP → ADP + Pi), energy can be released to power cellular work.

What ATP powers

  • Transport work: pumping ions/molecules across membranes.
  • Chemical work: building molecules (anabolism).
  • Mechanical work: movement (muscle contraction, motor proteins).

How ATP is regenerated

Cells regenerate ATP mainly through cellular respiration (and photosynthesis in plants). Because cells use ATP continuously, regeneration is constant.

Key takeaways

  • ATP hydrolysis releases usable energy.
  • ATP powers transport, chemical, and mechanical work.
  • ATP must be continuously regenerated.

Quick check

  1. What happens when ATP is hydrolyzed?

    Show answer

    ATP becomes ADP + phosphate and releases energy.

  2. Name one type of work powered by ATP.

    Show answer

    Transport work, chemical work, or mechanical work.

title: Quiz — Chapter 4 (6 questions)
content_html:
Quiz — Chapter 4: Metabolism & Energy

Answer the questions, then open the answers to self-check.

  1. Catabolic reactions generally:

    • Build large molecules
    • Release energy by breaking molecules down
    • Do not involve enzymes
    • Always require ATP input
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Release energy by breaking molecules down

    Catabolism breaks down molecules; energy can be captured.

  2. Enzymes speed reactions by:

    • Increasing activation energy
    • Lowering activation energy
    • Changing products
    • Changing equilibrium
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Lowering activation energy

    They make it easier for substrates to reach the transition state.

  3. The active site is:

    • Where substrates bind
    • Where DNA is stored
    • A membrane pump
    • A type of carbohydrate
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Where substrates bind

    The active site binds substrates and catalyzes the reaction.

  4. ATP is mainly used to:

    • Store long-term energy like fat
    • Provide immediate usable energy
    • Create isotopes
    • Break hydrogen bonds in DNA only
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Provide immediate usable energy

    ATP is a short-term energy currency for cellular work.

  5. Anabolic pathways usually:

    • Require energy input
    • Release large amounts of energy
    • Destroy proteins
    • Occur only in bacteria
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Require energy input

    Building molecules needs energy.

  6. Competitive inhibition:

    • Binds the active site
    • Binds only DNA
    • Always increases enzyme activity
    • Requires sunlight
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Binds the active site

    Competitive inhibitors compete with substrate for the active site.

title: 1.1 — What Is Biology? The Properties of Life
content_html:
Quiz — Chapter 3: The Organization of Cells

Answer the questions, then open the answers to self-check.

  1. Which is true of prokaryotes?

    • They have a nucleus
    • They have membrane-bound organelles
    • Their DNA is not in a nucleus
    • They are always multicellular
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Their DNA is not in a nucleus

    Prokaryotes lack a nucleus.

  2. Rough ER is associated with:

    • Lipid synthesis
    • Protein synthesis for export/membranes
    • DNA replication
    • ATP production
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Protein synthesis for export/membranes

    Rough ER has ribosomes that synthesize proteins.

  3. The Golgi apparatus mainly:

    • Stores genetic information
    • Modifies and sorts proteins
    • Breaks down waste (always)
    • Performs photosynthesis
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Modifies and sorts proteins

    Golgi processes and packages cargo into vesicles.

  4. Microtubules are important for:

    • Cell division and transport tracks
    • Making ATP
    • DNA base pairing
    • Buffering pH
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Cell division and transport tracks

    They form spindle fibers and serve as tracks for motor proteins.

  5. Facilitated diffusion:

    • Uses ATP
    • Moves molecules against gradient
    • Uses membrane proteins but no ATP
    • Only moves water
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Uses membrane proteins but no ATP

    Movement is down the gradient; proteins help crossing.

  6. Endocytosis is:

    • Moving ions through channels
    • Exporting vesicles out
    • Importing material using vesicles
    • Diffusion of water
    Answer + explanation

    Correct: Importing material using vesicles

    Endocytosis brings material into the cell.
